content.text: (formerly Integromat) are no-code/low-code automation platforms that connect 1000+ SaaS apps. Both have added native AI capabilities — OpenAI GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini — that let you embed LLM calls directly inside automation workflows without managing API keys or prompt infrastructure yourself.

attrs.alt: Zapier vs Make: trigger → AI step → filter/router → action, plus AI Agent modules on both platforms

content.text: an automated workflow. Each Zap has one Trigger and one or more Actions connected in a linear chain.
the event that starts the Zap (new email, form submission, scheduled time, incoming webhook).
a task Zapier performs (send email, create row, POST to API). Each Action step can reference output data from any earlier step.
conditional logic that stops the Zap from continuing unless a condition is met.
a branching step that sends the Zap down different paths based on conditions — equivalent to if/else.
a built-in step that calls an LLM (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini) with a prompt you define. Field references like
a visual flow built from modules connected by routes. Scenarios run on a schedule or when triggered by a webhook.
one node in the scenario — analogous to a Zap step. Each module has typed inputs/outputs mapped with Make's drag-and-drop formula editor.
a module that splits the flow into multiple independent paths, each with its own filter condition.
the Iterator unwraps an array into individual items; the Aggregator collects per-item results back into one bundle.
Make's built-in key-value database, useful for persisting memory across scenario runs (e.g., conversation history).
a first-class Make module for GPT completions, embeddings, image generation (DALL-E), and speech-to-text (Whisper). No custom HTTP calls needed.
trigger lets any external service start a Zap by sending an HTTP POST. Zapier exposes a unique catch-hook URL.

# Zapier Catch Hook (Webhook Trigger) — your app POSTs here
# In Zapier: Trigger → Webhooks by Zapier → Catch Hook
# Zapier provides a unique URL like:
#   https://hooks.zapier.com/hooks/catch/1234567/abcdef/

import requests

zapier_webhook_url = "https://hooks.zapier.com/hooks/catch/1234567/abcdef/"

payload = {
    "customer_email": "alice@example.com",
    "order_id":       "ORD-8821",
    "amount":         149.99,
    "currency":       "USD",
}
response = requests.post(zapier_webhook_url, json=payload)
print(response.status_code)  # 200 on success

Send the webhook a representative payload once when setting up the trigger — Zapier uses it to infer the data structure and expose field names for later steps. Update the sample if your payload schema changes.
anywhere in a Zap. Reference upstream field values in the prompt using
syntax. The step returns structured or unstructured text you can route downstream.

# Example: Zapier Zap with AI step
# Zap structure:
#   1. Trigger: New row in Google Sheets (columns: customer_name, complaint_text)
#   2. AI Step (GPT-4o): Classify the complaint severity (low/medium/high)
#                        and draft a reply in <50 words.
#   3. Filter: Only continue if severity == "high"
#   4. Action: Create a Zendesk ticket with the AI-drafted reply
#
# AI Step prompt in Zapier (references Sheets columns via {{...}}):
# ---
# You are a customer support classifier.
# Customer: {{customer_name}}
# Complaint: {{complaint_text}}
#
# Respond with JSON:
# {
#   "severity": "low" | "medium" | "high",
#   "reply": "<50-word empathetic reply>"
# }
# ---
# Zapier parses the JSON keys as output variables: {{severity}}, {{reply}}
if the AI returns JSON, Zapier automatically parses its top-level keys as separate output variables — no custom code needed.
GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Flash (model list varies by plan and region).
Zapier truncates AI step prompts at ~4000 tokens for the prompt alone. Send long documents via a pre-processing step that chunks or summarises first.
Zapier Canvas lets you build a
that can call your existing Zap actions as tools. The agent appears in a chat interface and is also accessible via a REST API.

# Zapier AI Agent — call via REST API
# In Zapier Canvas: Create an AI Agent → Settings → Enable API Access
# The agent has access to any Zap actions you've connected as tools.

import requests

AGENT_ID  = "your-agent-id"
API_TOKEN = "Bearer your-zapier-personal-access-token"

response = requests.post(
    f"https://actions.zapier.com/api/v2/ai-agents/{AGENT_ID}/chat",
    headers={"Authorization": API_TOKEN, "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={
        "message": "Check whether order ORD-8821 has shipped and send Alice an update email.",
        "conversation_id": "",   # empty to start a new conversation
    },
)
data = response.json()
print(data["response"])
print("tools_used:", data.get("tools_used", []))
any Zap action (Send Email, Create Ticket, Look Up Row) can be added as an agent tool. The LLM decides when to call each tool.
the agent maintains conversation history within a session. Cross-session memory requires a connected data source (Google Sheet, Airtable).
assign a system prompt in the Canvas settings to give the agent a role, tone, and constraints.
trigger in Make works identically: Make provides a URL, your app POSTs to it, and the scenario fires. Make can optionally return a synchronous response from the scenario.

# Make (Webhooks module) — receive an HTTP call and start a scenario
# In Make: Scenario → Add Trigger → Webhooks → Custom Webhook
# Make gives you a URL like:
#   https://hook.eu1.make.com/abcdefghij1234567890

import requests

make_webhook_url = "https://hook.eu1.make.com/abcdefghij1234567890"

payload = {
    "user_id":  "u-99",
    "question": "What is the best way to learn vector databases?",
}
resp = requests.post(make_webhook_url, json=payload)
# If the scenario's final module is a Webhook Response module:
print(resp.json())  # {"answer": "..."}
Make's native OpenAI module handles auth, retries, and response parsing for you. Reference module outputs using

# Make scenario — OpenAI module call
# In Make UI: Modules → OpenAI (GPT-3, DALL-E, Whisper) → Create a Completion
#
# Module settings:
#   Model: gpt-4o
#   Max tokens: 500
#   Messages:
#     System: "You are a senior Python code reviewer. Be concise."
#     User:   {{1.diff}}   ← references the output of module 1 (Webhook trigger)
#
# Make stores the response in: {{N.choices[].message.content}}
# where N is the module position number.
#
# Chaining: next module (e.g. Gmail → Send Email) references
#   Subject: "Code review for PR #{{1.pr_number}}"
#   Body:    {{3.choices[].message.content}}
module implements a ReAct planning loop. The agent has access to any Make module you configure as a tool — HTTP requests, native app connectors, or data stores.

# Make AI Agent module — ReAct loop with Make modules as tools
# In Make: Modules → AI Agent → Configure
#
# Agent settings:
#   Model: GPT-4o / Claude 3.5 Sonnet / Gemini 1.5
#   System Prompt: "You are a sales assistant with access to CRM tools."
#   Tools (each tool is a Make module connection):
#     - "get_customer" → HTTP module → GET https://crm.example.com/customers/{{id}}
#     - "create_deal"  → HTTP module → POST https://crm.example.com/deals
#     - "send_email"   → Gmail module → Send Email
#   Memory: "Conversation Memory" → stores up to 20 turns in session variables
#   Max iterations: 8
#
# Input to the agent: {{trigger.user_message}}
# Output from the agent: {{agent.response}}  ← ready for next module
Make's Iterator allows you to process arrays emitted by an LLM (e.g., a list of tasks, a set of search results) one item at a time without writing loop logic.
Both platforms support configurable retry and error handling. In Make, each module can have an attached error-handler route.

# Make error handling — resume or rollback on module failure

# In Make scenario editor:
# 1. Click a module that might fail (e.g., an HTTP call to an LLM API)
# 2. Right-click → Add error handler
# 3. Choose:
#    - Ignore:  skip the failed module, continue with the next
#    - Break:   stop this route, optionally retry N times with backoff
#    - Rollback: undo all committed operations in the scenario (for atomic flows)
#    - Commit:  finish this route, start a new transaction

# For LLM API rate limits:
# Add a Break handler with:
#   Number of attempts: 3
#   Interval between attempts: 60 seconds
# Make automatically retries the module before marking the run as failed.
to re-run failed tasks automatically after a delay. Premium plans support per-step error paths.
per-module error handlers (Ignore, Break, Rollback, Commit) give fine-grained control. Break with retry is ideal for LLM API rate-limit errors.
OpenAI module + HTTP for other LLMs
each AI Step in Zapier consumes one task
, and the LLM call itself counts as another task on some plans. Check your plan's AI task pricing before building high-volume zaps.
asking an LLM to return JSON and then parsing it downstream is brittle. Add explicit prompt constraints (
"Respond ONLY with valid JSON, no prose"
) and a JSON validation step to catch malformed output early.
Make's HTTP module caps response size at 5 MB. Large LLM outputs (long documents, base64-encoded files) require a webhook response with streaming or a pre-processing step to chunk output.

an AI Step inside a Paths branch fires independently for each path that is active. If three paths are active, the LLM is called three times. Hoist the AI step before the Paths step if the output is shared.

neither platform persists conversation memory across distinct sessions by default. Use Make Data Store, Google Sheets, or Airtable as an external memory store; look up history at the start of each scenario run.

Make runs scenarios synchronously by default. If your OpenAI calls hit the API rate limit, add a Break error handler with 3 retries and a 60-second interval rather than building sleep logic in a Code module.
Calling Custom LLM Endpoints (Make HTTP Module)
When the native OpenAI module does not cover your model or provider (e.g., Anthropic directly, a LiteLLM proxy, or a self-hosted Ollama), use Make's
module with a raw JSON body.

# Make HTTP Module calling a custom LLM endpoint (e.g., LiteLLM proxy)
# Use when the native OpenAI module doesn't support your model or provider.
# In Make: Modules → HTTP → Make a Request

# HTTP module settings:
#   URL:    https://your-litellm-proxy.example.com/v1/chat/completions
#   Method: POST
#   Headers:
#     Authorization: Bearer {{connection.api_key}}
#     Content-Type:  application/json
#   Body (raw JSON):
#     {
#       "model": "anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
#       "messages": [
#         {"role": "system",  "content": "You are a concise summariser."},
#         {"role": "user",    "content": "{{1.document_text}}"}
#       ],
#       "max_tokens": 400
#     }
#
# Parse the response with Make's JSON parser module:
#   {{N.choices[].message.content}}  → first choice text
store the API key in a Make Connection (Settings → Connections → OAuth 2.0 or API Key) and reference it as
module after the HTTP module to extract nested fields like
add a Break error handler to the HTTP module with 3 retries at 60-second intervals for 429 rate-limit responses.
LLM outputs are free-form text by default. For any downstream branching or data storage, enforce JSON output in the prompt and add a parsing/validation step immediately after the AI module.
Models sometimes wrap JSON output in markdown code fences (
). Strip them before parsing — either in a Code step or with a Make String Transform (
Both Zapier and Make can implement Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) by embedding the user question, querying a vector store, and injecting the retrieved chunks into the LLM prompt.
Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector. All expose REST APIs accessible from Make's HTTP module or Zapier's Webhook action.
is very cheap (< $0.001 / 1M tokens). Embed queries at query time — do not cache embeddings per user session unless queries repeat.
keep retrieved chunks under 500 tokens each and limit to 3–5 chunks to stay within the LLM's effective context window for question-answering.
Zapier builds linear automation with Triggers, Actions, Filters, and Paths; the
lets any step call GPT-4o, Claude, or Gemini with prompt templates using upstream field references.
creates conversational AI agents that call Zap actions as tools and expose a REST API endpoint.
Make (Integromat) uses a visual DAG of modules; the native
handles auth and parsing, and the
runs a ReAct loop with Make modules as tools.
pattern handles LLM-generated arrays without code; the
provides multi-path branching with per-path filters.
Both platforms support webhook-based APIs — Zapier's Catch Hook for async ingestion, Make's Custom Webhook with optional synchronous response for request-reply patterns.
Manage cost by hoisting shared AI steps before branching, adding JSON validation to catch malformed LLM output early, and configuring Break error handlers for rate-limit retries.
